text: If you want a successful front office, both the head coach and the GM need to drop their egos off at the door and feed on the same philosophy.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: Nearly everyone has a dot on our draft board every year. Ninety percent of anyone who has ever strapped on shoulder pads has had some type of tear in his labrum. Successful drafting means being able to trust your doctors.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: A good general manager is able to overrule coaches on personnel acquisitions. Too many won't or can't. A good coach understands and finds a way to win regardless.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Sure, luck means a lot in football. Not having a good quarterback is bad luck.
author: Don Shula

text: Promotion has become, in many ways, as important as the game itself. As a result, you promote your big-name players, your quarterback, and the fans want to see them play.
author: Sam Wyche

text: My job is to defeat the guy in front of me, do it until he quits, and then wait for them to send in the next guy.
author: Ndamukong Suh

text: How many times have I seen a guy knocked off our draft board because of an injury concern, and he has turned out to be a great player? At some point doctors need to be held accountable.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: When you look at some of the teams in the league that have dropped the most, you're often looking at a team that lost its defensive coordinator to a head-coaching job.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: You can play hard. You can play aggressive. You can give 120%, but if one guy is out of position then someone's running through the line of scrimmage and he's going to gain a bunch of yards.
author: Bill Belichick

text: In practice, you can only simulate so much stuff. When you're live, things are different. You can't always drop back, set your feet, and throw the ball. You've got to be able to move around back there and make people miss and find your guys.
author: Jake Delhomme

text: What do you think the hit ratio is on one-year college football wonders in the first round? We did the study over five years. It's not very good.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Sometimes, you look at what's coming in the draft, and you think you might rather be 2-14 than 6-10. But every team goes into the week trying to win.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: The West Coast offense is built around one position. All the production goes into the guy running underneath coverages. You move a guy into another position and he's still the same player, but everyone starts calling him a one-year guy.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: Now that there are eight divisions, winning one of those three in-division road games can make your season. Win two, and you've got yourself a great team.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: I want a safety who can be an extra defender in the box, go out and cover the slot in nickel situations and have the range to drop and play the deep ball. There are only three of those guys in the league right now, and maybe there's one in any given draft.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: A quarterback must learn how not to lose before he can grasp winning.
author: Adam Trask, Pittsburgh Ironmen

text: When you draft a player you draft a human being and you get to know these guys and you know how passionate they are and how badly they want to play. It tugs on you pretty hard.
author: Ted Thompson

text: Quarterbacks generally play well until they don't. And then they shouldn't.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: Football is a chess game to me. If you move your pawn against my bishop, I'll counter that move to beat you. Football is the same way. I study so much film that I know exactly what teams are going to do. I love knowing what an offense is going to run and stuffing that play.
author: Junior Seau

text: People say we haven't signed big free agents, but they lose sight of the fact that we signed significant players of our own.
author: Kevin Colbert

text: There is only so much talent available. It's important to know what traits you value the most in players, and what you can win with.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: Impressed by a lot of passing yards? Did you know that quarterbacks throwing for more than 350 yards are 462-464-3 since 1974. Passing yardage alone tells only a small part of the story.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: That team never keeps their draft picks healthy. When I see that, I see a doctor who can't tell the difference between an injury and a career-ending issue. Everyone gets injured at some point in college.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: A good defensive coordinator loves seeing a little guy in the backfield. Runners who cannot block better be able to make it in the slot because they will be exposed quickly.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Some quarterbacks can improvise, throw from odd angles, different release spots every time. That makes defense exponentially tougher.
author: Nick Carraway, Coach, Buffalo Snow Riders

text: Every battle is won before it is even fought.
author: Sun Tzu

text: It's a passing league. There is a premium now on big-play receivers. I think you'll see guys that can run fast start getting drafted earlier even if they don't seem to be football players.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: It takes three games to evaluate a quarterback. Three games, and a good defensive coordinator knows what they can and cannot do.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Everyone makes mistakes. There are no virgins in this business. It takes a lot of confidence to admit your mistakes and field the best 53 players.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: Left tackles are difficult to find. I don't think you can draft tackles and cornerbacks by always picking low in each round.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: It all goes to slow motion when you get there. You don't see anything but the quarterback. You don't hear anything but the quarterback's breath. It's almost like you're a shark. Your eyes get real big and everything's just quiet.
author: Mario Williams

text: The hardest position to fill in free agency is wide receiver. There are so many bad misses. It's difficult to project how a receiver will adjust to a new offense.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: Find out what the other team wants to do. Then take it away from them.
author: George Halas

text: As athletes, we are important celebrities, in demand and rich. Then we are out of the game and we are not important, not celebrities, not in demand and not rich.
author: Fran Tarkenton

text: Receivers are defined by the routes they run.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: You win on defense with talent. You win on offense with coaching. If you have good defensive players and you cannot win, you probably shouldn't be coaching in this league.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: A lot of times I find that people who are blessed with the most talent don't even develop that attitude, and the ones who aren't blessed in that way are the most competitive and have the biggest heart.
author: Tom Brady

text: There's too much money going to players at the top of the draft. You can't trade those picks because no one wants to pay for them unless they know exactly what they're getting in the draft.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: There are a lot of bad guys in the league. You can't have 53 choirboys on your roster. It has never been that way.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: A lot of it is having to throw when you're behind, but nothing correlates more to losing football than not completing your passes. Quarterbacks who fail to complete 20 or more passes in a game are 278-1435-7 since 1974. That's 16% wins.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: You want to know what makes the league? It's franchise quarterbacks. They put fans in the seats. They win championships. They sell the game.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: If you're going to rely on the 3-4, you need that two-gap nose tackle who forces two offensive linemen to pay attention. They're huge, and there's not always a good one in an entire draft class. That's why we don't run the 3-4.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: Offensive linemen can go out in shorts. It all starts with mental preparation. It's knowing who to block and technique and footwork.
author: Joey Berglund, Coach, Tennessee Raftsmen

text: I never like to be too optimistic about contracts because you're really not close until you sign them. Sometimes you can get to the brink and never get it done.
author: Ernie Accorsi

text: It is the best feeling in the world to have a close game come down to just a couple of plays and you are able to do it.
author: Drew Bledsoe

text: Many coaches can't adapt to their talent. Personnel and coaching don't always mix, and that's too big a problem for an average coach to overcome.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: The older I get, the better I used to be.
author: John McEnroe

text: Why is the spread offense so popular in both the college and pro game these days? It's a numbers game. You can't find enough good offensive linemen in college football, and the spread makes the game a lot easier for the big boys.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: The only groups organized team activities really help are the quarterback and the receivers getting their timing and routes in rhythm.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: It is best to weigh the enemy more mighty than he seems.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: My kingdom for a quarterback.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: The Tampa-2 has fallen out of favor. It's too easy to pick apart. Good quarterbacks find the soft spots and dink-and-dunk it to death.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Football is a sport of paradox. It requires reaction, not reflection. Yet you must use your mind to calculate, to anticipate - to think and not think at the same time.
author: Dhani Jones

text: Too many players make teams because of where they were drafted.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Don't coach scared. Don't sit back in soft cover-2. You won't get beat deep, but you won't get your offense on the field, either.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: On any given Sunday, any team in the league can beat any other team.
author: Bert Bell

text: You can watch a quarterback's footwork and tell whether he is accurate or not. The good quarterbacks shift their weight like a pitcher.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: Too many talent evaluators base their evaluations on what role a player is in rather than his value in that role. This leads to mediocre players receiving big free-agent contracts.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: With the salary cap, you can't make a mistake when it comes to identifying the players you can't do without. I'd rather let a $20 million player go and see him succeed elsewhere than spend $20 million for an ordinary guy.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: Someone told me the other day that he knows everything he needs to know about a player based on how fast he can run the three-cone drill. So I asked him what he did for a living. He said he sold ice cream out of a truck.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: A quarterback needs to be the toughest player on the team, mentally and physically. That's what the great ones share.
author: Adam Trask, Pittsburgh Ironmen

text: Stability in coaching is underrated. You don't want to lose your coordinators. The best coordinator is someone you know will stick around.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: It all starts with the defensive line, especially in the 4-3. That's where you get your pressure. And against the run, they have to contain the linemen so your linebackers can make tackles. A linebacker can't tackle if there's a 320-pound set of shoulder pads in his face.
author: Nick Carraway, Coach, Buffalo Snow Riders

text: He only shows up when he wants to. He's the type of guy who makes you want to pound your head against the wall, because when he does show up, he makes a difference. And then he doesn't.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: It is one thing to be tempted by the quarterback's eyes. It is another to break for a pass that never comes.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: Too many coaches only know certain schemes. They know power running or zone running, West Coast or vertical. The league is too scheme-specific.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Football is like life. It requires perseverance, self-denial, hard-work, sacrifice, dedication and respect for authority.
author: Vince Lombardi

text: I can explain why a big guy with great speed and hands doesn't always succeed in this league. We ask receivers to memorize the complex parts of the playbook and run good routes. I would take a smart receiver over a fast one.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: I look at this player, and I think there's no way on earth anyone could tell me he belonged on this roster if he wasn't drafted. No one would fight for him if he was a free agent rather than a second-round pick.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Having quickness on the line is so important inside. It's hard to rush the passer with power. Everyone in the league has power on the lines. It is all about speed.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: You need to understand situational football and have more than just a gut feel for why things will work. You'd be surprised how unprepared some coaches are coming into games.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Do what you feel in your heart to be right - for you'll be criticized anyway. You'll be damned if you do, and damned if you don't.
author: Eleanor Roosevelt

text: Too many owners don't understand that evaluating talent is just as important as hiring a good coach.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: All that is valuable in human society depends upon the opportunity for development accorded the individual.
author: Albert Einstein

text: What a lot of fans, executives and even owners do not realize is how fine the line is between winning and losing in this league. Blowing up the building is not always the answer.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: More and more teams are using almost exclusively the draft to build their teams. And that means you have younger players to develop in those key depth positions.
author: Ozzie Newsome

text: With the new CBA, teams are not going to get strangled by a rookie contract for $50 million. That's how the league was built - trying to level the playing field so everyone has a chance every year.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: A poor player struts and frets his hour upon the stage and then is heard no more. His tale is told by an idiot, full of sound and fury, signifying nothing.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: There are 32 teams and maybe a couple dozen quarterbacks who can play the modern game. Great coaches can win without a quarterback, but it isn't easy.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: A good decision is based on knowledge and not on numbers.
author: Plato

text: Teams are doing better locking up their own talent. There's not much available in free agency these days.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: You don't need a great offensive line to win. You need five tough, smart, durable guys who build a cohesive unit. Durability is more important than getting that $20 million guy.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: The heartbeat of a football team is the quarterback position and I think everyone who has any intelligence about the game understands you must have consistency at that position to be a championship team.
author: Ron Jaworski

text: Pressure is something you feel when you don't know what the hell you're doing.
author: Peyton Manning

text: The toughest thing in sports has nothing to do with hitting the curveball. It's evaluating quarterbacks.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: They didn't let just anybody in that club. Took every ounce of heart and sweat and blood. To get to wear those game-day jerseys down the hall. The kings of the school, man, we're the boys of fall.
author: Kenny Chesney

text: Worry about the guys who put it all together in a contract year. Intensity and motor will always be issues.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: No excuses. No explanations. Turn the page.
author: Tony Dungy

text: When it comes to the draft, finding a great fit is much more important at the end of the draft. If you're thinking about fit early on, you're probably about to make a mistake.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: Don't set your players out to fail. This game is not nearly as difficult as a lot of young coaches and coordinators make it out to be.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: They're giving the cover corners an even smaller window than they had before. Now the guys who play hard at the corner get flagged all the time. The great cover corner is absolutely rare and special.
author: Scott Pioli

text: All the code that actually matters in the Front Office Football game engine was copied from page 401 of the April 1941 issue of BYTE magazine.
author: Jim Gindin

text: Who do you think you are? I am!
author: Pete Weber

text: Quarterback is the most important position on the field. You need to have a plan or a short list at all times. You can't wait until there's an injury to know who is available.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: Do you draft for need or do you draft the best player on the board? If you look at the best drafts, they are always looking for the best player at priority positions.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: There is too much buzz about the underclassmen. Scouts know the seniors and tend to focus on what they can't do well. With the juniors, scouts get too excited about what they can do well.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: What you see in places that are winning right now are coaches and GMs who are willing to take some chances on talent with issues. Stay on them, though. Manage them and help them develop or move on.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: You catch some bad breaks, no doubt. But even some championship teams have had a lot of guys on injured reserve. The good ones adapt. You look for excuses in this league, and you're done.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: Since 1978, 23% of all league games have been won by three points or less, and 48% by eight points or less. Every play means something.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: Man is most nearly himself when he achieves the seriousness of a child at play.
author: Heraclitus

text: You have to throw to win. More teams are throwing because you can't hit the quarterback or touch the receivers. The league wants teams to air it out. No one lines up and knocks you off the ball these days.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: Any defensive coordinator is worried about two things: a running quarterback and a deep ball.
author: John Madden

text: There are too many insecure personnel people in this league. Their vision gets narrow and they can't see what's about to hit them.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: Smart veterans stick around even when they no longer have what they used to have. Smart GMs don't let smart veterans out of the building.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Quarterbacks take a lot of blame because young receivers are seldom in the right spot when the quarterback hits his fifth step. So he has to hold the ball too long and that breaks the play.
author: Adam Trask, Pittsburgh Ironmen

text: Situational analysis reeks of mojo and small sample sizes.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: Show me the money!
author: Rod Tidwell (Jerry Maguire)

text: It's not about collecting talent, it's about building a team.
author: Bill Belichick

text: Every player is damaged, somehow. The trick is finding the damage that won't hurt you.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: The problem with the Tampa-2 defense. If you don't have six or seven all-stars on the field, it's not going to work. When you don't disguise your coverages, it's just like taking candy from a baby.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: As a football player, you just deal with the injuries. It's all part of the football game. I've dealt with injuries as much as everybody else. People have dealt with worse injuries than I've dealt with. It's all part of the game, all part of getting that tackle.
author: Troy Polamalu

text: Too many teams are emotionally attached to their veterans. Judge players for what they can offer your team, not for what they used to offer your team.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: We project body shaping and size, especially weight, and growth potential when we evaluate a player and project them. We make sure we know the correct date of birth and we do a lot of research to verify it.
author: Ernie Accorsi

text: In the old days, you'd try and punish the quarterback. Now there are rules against it, and we're seeing more and more quarterbacks getting better and better after a few years in the league. That may be good for the game, but it's definitely driving up the scores.
author: Nick Carraway, Coach, Buffalo Snow Riders

text: Bad drafters spend their time worrying about where players will go. If you evaluate properly, it makes no difference. You'll know the best player when he's still there and he's still on your board.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: If you let the fans dictate your choices, you'll wind up sitting next to them.
author: Marv Levy

text: Defensive linemen normally get a little bit more publicity but I think if you have a chance to draft a good, solid core-type guy, you better take him.
author: Ted Thompson

text: You get a couple of good, strong, north-south runners in there and you run them until they're no longer effective. Most running backs aren't going to last more than three years. They take too many hits.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: One day you're anointed the king of the league and the next day everyone wants to fire you. It's a very competitive league.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: The value of running backs is going down. It takes more than one back to win in this league. I see more and more teams going with a committee approach, realizing you can't have enough healthy backs.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: The game changes in many ways. Ten years ago, we wanted players who never told us when they were hurt. Trainers were paid to keep them on the field. Today, we need to be more concerned about the long-term effects of injuries.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: Don't put your players in positions where they can't succeed.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: You can always have a discussion with a guy who watches tape. You can discuss what you see and why things are happening and maybe come together in areas where one person did not have knowledge. If one watches the tape and the other does not, you are having an argument.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: Find good football players on the first day. Find guys who will fit anywhere. Find guys you can count on lining up in any scheme and making an impact.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: They say it's worth taking a penalty to keep the quarterback from taking a big hit. They also say that the only linemen the fans love are the linemen who never take penalties. There may be five of us, but sometimes it's a lonely job.
author: John Wheelwright, Houston Apollos

text: Football combines two of the worst things in American life. It is violence punctuated by committee meetings.
author: George Will

text: If your quarterback can consistently and wisely get the ball out in less than two seconds, defenses have to commit to coverage, and that opens up everything.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: We run the 4-3, so we look for outside linebackers who can get to the sidelines quickly. But it's a numbers game, and they have to be big enough to deal with an occasional offensive lineman.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: Teams are made up of a lot of components. They're made up of hunger, they're made up of desire, they're made up of chemistry, and they're made up of emotion.
author: H.G. Bissinger

text: How do you play quarterback when every time you drop, you get hit in the mouth and your receivers are not where they are supposed to be?
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: You need to know the difference between mischief and criminals. You want guys who have been in fights. You don't want guys who steal or guys you can't trust.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: A good quarterback never blames his receiver, especially not in front of any audience. Even when the receiver is to blame, the cost of assigning that blame is far too great.
author: Adam Trask, Pittsburgh Ironmen

text: Some guys can be a leader and be a running back or a lineman, or a wide receiver, strong safety, or linebacker. But when you speak of quarterbacks, it's automatically a default that you're supposed to be a leader.
author: Cam Newton

text: The problem with most personnel departments is that they don't understand the basic rules of the business. They don't understand the concept of street value for free agents or for draft picks. So they waste a lot of time they should be spending on the tough decisions.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: The best evaluators are blue-collar guys. You don't hear about them a lot. Fans don't know their names. They are not in the headlines. They don't want attention. And so they don't get the opportunities they deserve to run teams.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: It blows me away how many teams I see in a two-deep against trips. That is Football 101. You have to check out of it.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Let me explain my job very simply: my job is to line up five, seven, ten yards in front of a man and run into him at full speed.
author: Ray Lewis

text: Fans don't want to believe what scouts say about running backs. They get hypnotized by statistics. There's no such thing as a Hall of Fame running back. Offensive linemen decide the greatness of the men who carry the ball.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: The brightest decision-makers are always looking left and looking right and understand that any piece of information can be beneficial. Information always helps make good decisions.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: If you're a quarterback, you want everything on your shoulders. You want to be the one to make the decisions.
author: Tom Brady

text: I measure the success of an offense very differently. All I look at is how often they go three-and-out. Nothing else matters. Just look into the eyes of your defensive linemen after your offense goes three-and-out and tell me anything else.
author: Nick Carraway, Coach, Buffalo Snow Riders

text: Since we went to the modern eight-division format, 34% of our 9-7 teams reached the playoffs, 82% of our 10-6 teams and 97% of our 11-5 teams. It's a fair system. You're looking for ten wins out of the gate.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: You have to be able to stop the run and put teams in situations where you know they have to throw the ball. You build from the front back, not from the back forward. History supports this concept, regardless of all the spread offenses and other gimmicks you see out there.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: A mediocre linebacker has no virtue. He is a cloud upon your defense, wandering aimlessly sideline to sideline, chasing shadows.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: You see a guy who had two pick-sixes last year. I see a guy who gave up 20 touchdowns in single coverage.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: We will be perfect in every aspect of the game. You drop a pass, you run a mile. You miss a blocking assignment, you run a mile. You fumble the football, and I will break my foot off in your John Brown hind parts and then you will run a mile. Perfection.
author: Herman Boone (Remember the Titans)

text: You can't look past next week or you're dead in the water. There are no gimmes anymore.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: When you have a young quarterback, it's going to take some time to work out the growing pains.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Somehow, we managed to convince all these cities to build these grand new stadiums. Why? Well, the new stadiums work just as well as the old ones. But they have twice as many luxury boxes, and that's revenue teams don't have to share with other teams.
author: Patrick Wallingford, past league commissioner

text: Middle linebackers are like running backs. One or two big years, and it's all about reputation. Understandable when you consider both positions are all about getting pounded by people twice your size.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: Some receivers are slow and older than dirt, but they still get open. They're crafty, and they know how to set guys up.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: I thought he was overrated coming out of college, and he has done nothing to change my mind. He's nothing but a bunch of hot air, and I think people are starting to come around to it.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: If you're asking your corners to cover man-to-man for seven full seconds, well, there's not a guy in the league who can do that consistently. At least not against a receiver who is worth anything.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: There are two things every man in America thinks he can do: work a grill and coach football.
author: Greg Schiano

text: Everything is a function of money. If you give a blockhead a lot of money, he's going to become a bigger blockhead.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: There are always teams that start off the season hot. What everyone forgets is that the season is not a sprint. It's a marathon. This is a rough business.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: The center position is one of the hardest positions to grade because they are almost always uncovered, and they only stand out when they make mistakes.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Most coaches don't understand personnel. Yet in every personnel meeting, coaches outnumber GMs. Who do you think wins most of the battles?
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: I have an entire section of the playbook just for first down after a change in possession. These players set the tone. They have to be different. They have to be what we do best.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: You need a deep rotation on the defensive line, and you need players who can use their speed and size on every play to collapse the pocket and make the quarterback uncomfortable. We use a rotation because the threat has to be there on every play.
author: Fred Trumper, San Diego Lightning

text: The key to a winning season is focusing on one opponent at a time. Winning one week at a time. Never look back and never look ahead.
author: Chuck Noll

text: We see more zone blitzing these days. If we saw the same blitz 100 times, we could solve it. But the concepts themselves are multiplying. There are defensive coordinators creating packages that have evolved far beyond our solutions.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: If you can pressure the quarterback, you have a chance.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: Winning is a habit. Unfortunately, so is losing.
author: Vince Lombardi

text: Sometimes, you're in a position where you have to fire the head coach or you have to fire 53 players. You might love your coach and you might want to fire all 53 players, but you do what you have to do.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: When you have a veteran team and you start losing, it's more difficult to get those guys going again. They are more quick to shut it down.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: When you're 6-10 two years running, someone has to get fired. Never mind that there are 31 other teams trying to beat you. This is a competitive league and sometimes good people get fired for reasons entirely out of their control.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: Bad drafts don't hit immediately. You feel them years down the road. When you need those guys to step up in their third year and they don't, that is when the team can really start sliding.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: I love the zone blitz, but seconds feel like hours if they don't get there, because if you give a quarterback time to read the shift, he'll find someone wide open right where you don't want him.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Whenever you find yourself on the side of the majority, it is time to pause and reflect.
author: Mark Twain

text: You hear a lot about how a quarterback and his receivers need to be in synch. Same thing with the offensive line. Most sacks come because they are not playing together, not because one guy got beat.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Evaluators need to spend some more time talking to a player's position coach. They have insights you can't find on tape. Far too much money is handed out without doing the necessary background work.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: The only way players get better is to play. Running around in shorts does not do anything for me. There is no contact.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: In football everything is complicated by the presence of the opposite team.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: It's easy to draw it up and talk about it and teach it, but the players have to execute it. When you have a lot of rookies on your team, that's easier said than done.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: When you see a defense getting gouged through the air, more often than not they're not getting pressure up front. You give up far fewer deep plays when you have a solid defensive line.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: Victory always tastes sweeter when it's unexpected. Then again, sugar is bad for you. Find a way to expect victory and you'll find your tastes maturing.
author: Joey Berglund, Coach, Tennessee Raftsmen

text: Some quarterbacks are born great. Some achieve greatness. And some have greatness thrust upon them. But only a great coach recognizes greatness.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: What really stands out is how smart they are. The receivers run precise routes at the right lengths and know exactly where they are on the field. The quarterback is one of the smartest in the game.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: Our draft board gets screwed up every year after the coaches get involved.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: You always want to have good balance. To be able to run the ball effectively and throw the ball effectively is what gives you the chance to win a world championship.
author: John Elway

text: There just are not enough good quarterbacks to go around. It takes time to develop them, even when they have all the talent in the world.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Too many teams ignore why good players are let go. Free agency takes as much investigation as evaluating, and not everyone will be forthright, especially where there is a vested interest.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: What pushes them to be great? It's the taste of victory. They love that taste.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: I don't trade for players, I trade for potential. By the time a player is worth trading for, he's too expensive.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: Most running backs are never asked to block in college. If you don't see a good runner on the field as a rookie, it's because there's an adjustment period while they learn about blocking.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Just win, baby.
author: Al Davis

text: The wildest colts make the best horses.
author: Plutarch

text: Nobody's a genius in third-and-long. It's like admitting the secret ingredient in your awe-inspiring pork barbeque is 50-cent lite beer from the back of the cooler.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Fundamentals are getting worse in the college game. What's sexy right now is the innovator of something newfangled. Sound fundamentals aren't sexy.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: When you are running a 3-4, you better have the right pieces that are able to play a certain way, or it is not going to work.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: The fans seem to want more playoff teams so that ten-win teams are never excluded. But since we realigned, teams with less than ten wins are just about .500 in the playoffs. More playoff teams means more nine-win teams upsetting the best teams.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: The salary cap gives you about a three-year window before it starts catching up with you.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: Even if you have extra money, it still comes down to making good, sound football judgements. We made all our evaluations a while ago and we're not going to change a player's value because we have more money to spend.
author: Ernie Accorsi

text: Coaches have got to be able to get the most out of players and then take that hat off and look at them objectively when they watch tape.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: When you have five all-stars on your defense, anyone can be a great coach. The great coaches can keep a team competitive when the all-stars are not there. Not many can do it.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: The ability to assemble a solid coaching staff and manage it is one of the most difficult jobs of being a head coach.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: Every week is its own challenge. Every game brings its own set of circumstances, adjustments, play style, and matchups. We focus on what we want to do for that week, not what we did two weeks before or ten weeks before.
author: Bill Belichick

text: The reason for football is not to be the best but to be the best team.
author: Barry Sanders

text: Too many evaluators wouldn't know a good offensive lineman even if he lined up against him. And that's where it all starts with the offense.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: The quickest way for coaches to separate themselves is to make the lowest-rated guy into a starter. They thrive on it. They work a lot harder with a seventh-rounder than a first-rounder. It's one of the reasons why there are first-round busts.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: The problem with a lot of players that come into the league is that they have been the best player on their team since they started playing the game. They have been babied all their lives at every level. Players need to be held accountable.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: What we like about the 4-3 is that our defensive line can play more aggressively. We rarely ask them to play more than one gap. Just get into that gap and blow up the play.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: You see a lot of great physical specimens struggle, and the reason is that they have no idea where the ball is going.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: Ability will never catch up with the demand for it.
author: Confucius

text: Too many coaches get caught up in trying to pad their resumes and lead the league in categories instead of concentrating on winning football games. You can lead the league in scoring and still have a mediocre team.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: One of my greatest war-room speeches was about the virtue of the punter. I had charts, slide-shows, and I proved beyond the shadow of a doubt that a punter was twice as valuable as anyone thought he was. So we drafted one. And he was terrible.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: With all the money out there, players aren't going to sign in the last year of their contracts. They want to test the market. With the salary cap, you have to be aggressive about keeping your best players under contract.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: The power of accurate observation is commonly called cynicism by those who have not got it.
author: George Bernard Shaw

text: When I see a running quarterback, I see a clock ticking closer and closer to the day when he can't run anymore. But that doesn't make him any easier to prepare for.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Once the quarterback gets paid, the die is cast. That's what the salary cap was designed to do - limit the roster expenditures of good teams.
author: Bill Polian

text: I wouldn't be the man I am at all if I hadn't dropped the ball.
author: James Otto

text: I like to find receivers that may not have the quickness you see in the first round, but if you don't get your hands on them, you're done.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: The fans see a tight end who runs the seam like a gazelle, catches everything, breaks a bunch of tackles and scores 15 touchdowns, and they want me to go out and get one. Problem is, there are only a couple of them out there and they're already gotten.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Sacks are the greatest misconception in the league. There are so many good inside pass rushers that push everything out, and then the end gets the sack.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: Everything goes in cycles in this league. Teams have mostly figured out the cover-2, so you see more 3-4 defenses. But when teams figure that out, you'll see them reverting once again.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: The only thing I ever wanted to be was a professional football player.
author: Tom Brady

text: Great quarterbacks win games and reach the playoffs; great teams win playoff games.
author: Adam Trask, Pittsburgh Ironmen

text: Talent determines what you can do. Motivation determines what you're willing to do. Attitude determines how well you do it.
author: Lou Holtz

text: Is any free agent worth $20 million? No. Never. But it might be worth $20 million to avoid letting your biggest rival find out that no free agent is worth more than $15 million.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: Injuries are a part of the game. The number one goal of a strength and conditioning staff is to build a program that will keep players healthy.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: If you see a trend and you can't lead it, you're often better off trying something new. If everyone wants tweener end/linebackers so they can play a 3-4, unless you have those guys you should be playing a 4-3.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: You never think about trying to lose. You would lose the players if you did. When players are losing, they are miserable. They are fighting, scratching, to win because there is such a difference in how it feels to win and lose.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: Interceptions can change games, but I don't care much about whether my defensive backs have good hands. I want them preventing completions any way they can. Interceptions are terrific, but so are incompletions.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Coaches watch a few minutes of tape, go to an all-star game and a couple of workouts, spend some time with their friends and they have all the answers after a month of work. Most of them fall in love with players for all the wrong reasons.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: Many coaches can coach, but they can't teach. There's a difference. Teachers educate through a process. Coaches often holler and point and scream. Players don't respond well to hollering, and you don't see much development in those places.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: There's a lot of parity in this league. Since we've realigned 12 years ago, only 29 teams have had more than 12 wins during the regular season. Even more amazing is that those 29 teams have accounted for only 32 playoff victories.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: Monday morning is the winter of our discontent.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: The complete defensive end, and you might find one or two in the league at any time, can beat you inside with power, outside with speed, and has the lateral quickness to chase to the sideline and downfield. He can hold the point and stop the run.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Pro football is like nuclear warfare. There are no winners, only survivors.
author: Frank Gifford

text: In the past, coaches would help cover up a player's indiscretions off the field. People are finally willing to accept that winning isn't enough. You have to win and feel good about the people around you as well.
author: Nick Carraway, Coach, Buffalo Snow Riders

text: Do you want to upgrade your offensive line? Find a quarterback who can get the ball out so quickly that no one will notice how bad they are.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Rather than just putting another guy on the team, we're trying to make sure whoever we add will add something.
author: Ted Thompson

text: For a general manager, choosing a quarterback is a lot like life would be if you were forever 16, and the driver's test was written in Sanskrit on the back of a cocktail napkin dipped in napalm.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: As I look back on my career, I'm proud of only one thing: I'm told my name was always on the lips of our opponent's defensive coordinator the week before a game.
author: Gary Lambert, New Jersey Meteorites

text: If a team can put eight or nine in the box and not worry about you beating them with the pass, you can have Superman himself at running back and it won't matter a bit.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: I can spend hours breaking down a team's tendencies at every down and distance. But winning games means putting teams in the positions where everyone has to throw the ball. I might get one good play call out of learning tendencies. They change. Third-and-ten does not change.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: It's easier to know who to block when you are facing a four-man front, and that ultimately translates to better quarterback play.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: What's fun about cover-3 is that I have the numbers to blitz from either side. The quarterback has to read the shift. We can go sky or cloud from either side. And if he tries to throw into the blitz, we have a linebacker moving in the right direction.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: Too many coaches keep players two and three years too long because of their reputation.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: We learn from history that we do not learn from history.
author: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel

text: I'm looking at the quarterbacks in this league. Only six or seven are real playmakers. The rest are game managers. Most guys are going to get you there without making mistakes. They can't beat you, but they won't lose the game.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Your right defensive end is the best athlete on the field. He's going up against the left tackle, and he needs power, speed and great technique. And he needs to control his responsibility. You're in for a long day if they can get five or six behind that tackle.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: I try not to acquire the players we needed yesterday. It's better to anticipate and acquire the players we'll need tomorrow.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: To blitz, or not to blitz, that is the question.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: The pressure in this league is bad, at every level of the organization. You're always worried about losing your job, and the higher up you climb, the more is expected.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: What's the worst thing that can happen to a quarterback? He loses his confidence.
author: Terry Bradshaw

text: Offensive players take longer to develop. It's much easier to learn a defensive system than a 300-page playbook.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: If a guy is hurt all the time, he's not much help. You have to be durable to play in this league. You have to play through bumps and scrapes.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: I like statistics as much as the next guy. But this isn't baseball. The context of each statistic means more than the number itself. This is a team game, and numbers reflect game plans and what your teammates are doing just as much as they reflect the individual.
author: Joey Berglund, Coach, Tennessee Raftsmen

text: Your nickel back is pretty much a starter these days. It's a game of numbers, and some teams have pretty good athletes in the slot. Guys you can't jam, and they'll find openings in traffic if you let them.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Football is a game of errors. The team that makes the fewest errors in a game usually wins.
author: Paul Brown

text: If you know the enemy and know yourself you need not fear the results of a hundred battles.
author: Sun Tzu

text: He's dancing after he sacked the quarterback when they're down 28 in the fourth quarter, and that quarterback has already thrown for 300 and three touchdowns. What's he dancing about? The scouts from Dancing with the Stars went home 20 minutes ago.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Simple positional techniques like blocking and tackling and being a press corner are really sliding downhill in college. Too many college coaches spend all their time on Xs and Os, and trying to become the next great guru.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Don't let your better players out of the building without a good contract.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: There's a board game called Settlers of Catan. That is what I play. I am so embarrassed.
author: Andrew Luck

text: Leadership is the most overrated thing I ever heard of. There comes a time when you have to get rid of veterans. Everyone has to deal with it.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: You see tight ends out there catching 40 or 50 passes in a season. All I think about is what a tight end can do for me in terms of pass protection and run blocking. It's a lot easier to contain the edge if you don't have to worry about the tight end.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: Once you're a football player, you're a football player for life. You always think of yourself in terms of that. We all do. It's hard to get rid of when you can't play anymore.
author: Pete Carroll

text: Every time that darned bell rings during the combine, an agent gets his wings.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: The hardest part of a coach's job is determining when to fire a player.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: Our linebackers in the 3-4 are primarily pass rushers. They're fast and big enough to go up against the tackles. But we don't ask them to pursue like they would in a 4-3. One quick burst, but they generally stay on their side of the field.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: No one knows how to manage kickers. You can't talk to them. You can't avoid them. You just hope they never miss and then no one asks you to go out and find a new one.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: A foolish GM thinks himself to be wise, but a wise GM knows himself to be a fool.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: We went to the 16-game schedule about 35 years ago. So there's a lot of data. Teams rebound quickly. If you went 4-12 or worse in a season, you gained an average of 3.2 wins the following year. This keeps fans in the game long-term.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: These guys have to learn earlier what it means to be pros. You can't underestimate rest, rehab, nutrition, and on and on it goes. If you're not mature coming into this league, you're going to have a hard time adjusting.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: Too often, coaches watch tape together, and arrive at the same opinion. Originality is discouraged. I want to know what each coach sees, not what the dominant personality in the room thinks.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: The scary thing is that players have a one-upsmanship about money; they sign a contract and they like it until someone signs a bigger one and now they don't like it. I don't like that. I don't begrudge anyone money, but it disrupts the football team.
author: Joe Greene

text: It was an ideal day for football - too cold for the spectators and too cold for the players.
author: Red Smith

text: If you have your scouts spend a lot of time on one position, you can find starters in the late rounds. It does not matter where you draft them if you know what you're pursuing.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Offensive linemen don't dictate what a defense can and will try to do. That's the quarterback. You prepare for the quarterback and for elite skill players.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: Sure, it's impressive when a linebacker gets more than 100 tackles. But in some defenses, everything is geared toward keeping that linebacker free to make those tackles. Don't make the mistake of paying for something your coach's game plan already gets you.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: He can take his'n and beat your'n and your'n and beat his'n.
author: Bum Phillips

text: Mostly, agents just try and pad their own egos by demanding a few extra dollars at the end of a contract that everyone knows will be renegotiated or terminated long before completion. But every once in a while, one of them knows what he's doing.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: I want my wide receivers to have massive egos. I want every one of them to think they're the first guy in the history of football capable of making that play. Those guys sometimes are.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: If your starting quarterback tries to go on a year-long tuxedo tour while producing a .617 OPS and playing shortstop with the range of a lightly-deceased three-toed sloth, the fans are going to start screaming for his rookie backup at every turn. Baseball is a funny game.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: Defensive ends in the 3-4 aren't rock stars like they are in the 4-3, but don't underrate them. They have to control the line like a 4-3 defensive tackle, but they also have to be far more mobile.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: Those old nose tackles that get big and chubby and have played ten-plus years, they generally know right where the ball is going when it is snapped.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: There are 11 people out there playing defense. What makes a difference is how those 11 people play. That's what defense is about. It's about team defense. You are always trying to isolate it into one player, one situation or one thing, and it just doesn't work that way.
author: Bill Belichick

text: Sometimes, it's about how hard you hit a guy. And maybe in the first quarter, you want to make that kind of a statement. But if you leave your feet and you wind up missing him and he scrambles for a first down, you've probably done your team quite a bit of harm.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: The best time to enter free agency is after the first wave. You get in early and you have to pay too much. The market always pushes the price tag. You can find good value every year. You just have to go back to the tape and look hard.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: Too many defenders are looking for the big hit. The art of tackling has become lost in football. There are too many guys looking for the knockout shot and the highlight-reel hit and not enough that are playing good, solid, fundamental football.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: Everyone likes their draft picks in May. Players are always impressive in minicamps. I could care less. I want to see who comes ready to play when the pads go on.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: It's getting so that every team runs on a second down after an incompletion. It's like clearing your throat before you begin a speech. Lots of rattling, not much production.
author: Joey Berglund, Coach, Tennessee Raftsmen

text: The future belongs to those who believe in the beauty of their dreams.
author: Eleanor Roosevelt

text: To achieve any group goal, individuals must work to improve themselves as a means to improve the group.
author: George M. Gilbert

text: Today I will do what others won't, so tomorrow I can accomplish what others can't.
author: Jerry Rice

text: A cornerback who is afraid to make an impact on every play is something comparable to a sound like someone trying not to make a sound.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: Why give him $20 million in the fourth year when you know you'll never pay it out? Well, it spreads out the bonus money. It makes his agent really, really happy. And it doesn't cost you anything at all. It's just another three-year contract.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: Evaluating quarterbacks is a lot like going to the National Gallery and trying to find the best painting. There's an inner technique to all of them, and sometimes evaluation is just personal preference.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Uneasy lies the head that wears the coach's cap.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: Effort without talent is a depressing situation, but talent without effort is a tragedy.
author: Mike Ditka

text: He was a great defensive back. No one ever got behind him. He must have led the league in tackles 30 years behind the line of scrimmage. I think they renamed the buttonhook route in his honour. He'd never even blink at it. But he'd eventually tackle the guy.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Stepping up in the pocket and delivering a precise, confident strike is like doing the New York Times crossword in ink. But don't make a mistake. Instead of messing up your crossword, someone takes away your newspaper, hits you on the head with it, and scores a touchdown.
author: Adam Trask, Pittsburgh Ironmen

text: If you're not getting to the quarterback, eventually any Joe will find the holes in your coverage. If you have no holes in your coverage, obviously you're getting to the quarterback.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: I'm not sure why players don't wear full pads when they're running at the combine. Maybe that doesn't make a difference with the big guys, but we're making million-dollar decisions over one-tenth of a second with these little guys.
author: Nick Carraway, Coach, Buffalo Snow Riders

text: Look at some of the rookies stashed on injured reserve. Plenty of teams are too embarrassed to cut a high-round draft pick who simply cannot play the game.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: You can discover more about a person in an hour of play than in a year of conversation.
author: Plato

text: I like the spread because there's always a mismatch somewhere. But if they can get to your quarterback quickly, good decisions become a lot harder and you can't counter pressure as easily with the running game.
author: Gary Lambert, New Jersey Meteorites

text: Scoring is finally down a little but we've now had five straight years over 22 points per team per game. Before that, the last time we saw 22 was when we were figuring out how to merge two leagues together in the 1960s.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: The margin of error is so small. It can come down to hitting on one draft pick no one expects or finding one gem after the draft or in free agency. That can be the difference between making the playoffs and sitting at home.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: Good defensive tackles eat a lot of space. And they sit out a lot of plays. It's harder than it looks. They had better be in great shape, too. You can eat a lot of space and still be moved out of position.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: What you are hoping for is that the highest rated player is the position that you need. And that happens, it happens a lot.
author: Ernie Accorsi

text: If they played football by repeatedly yanking the quarterback over their heads, raising and lowering him, then finally slamming him to the turf. Then, and only then, I might start writing down those bench press scores during the combine.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: The best receivers know where the hole will be and when they want to get to that to time it up with the quarterback. The best receivers can change the speed of their routes.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: You are meant to play the ball as it lies, a fact that may help to touch on your own objective approach to life.
author: Grantland Rice

text: Sacks are nice, they're drive-killers. But I'd rather get five pressures on the quarterback than get one sack. Anything to keep him from having the time to break down our coverage.
author: Fred Trumper, San Diego Lightning

text: Why even use the solecismic test, then? I can't say it matters for every position, but we have these massive playbooks these days, and it takes book smarts to digest them properly. I want a good share of book-smart players on offense.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: If an agent comes to me, and all he can say is that his player started ten games last season, I don't have to look at the film to know what I'm going to see. Experience means a lot in this league, and when you're struggling to find a job it works against you.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: You see two types of fullbacks. There are the thumpers and there are the backs who block with their eyes. They don't knock anyone around, but they find a hole for the runner every time.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Oh, coach makes certain we know our Xs and Os before every game. The problem was that I Xed while he Oed.
author: John Berry, Green Bay Green Wave

text: If you can't contain the sidelines on defense, game-planning is like using your fingers to plug the holes while the dam breaks.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: You look at this quarterback and you don't see a single weakness. He can hit a target from 40 yards away. Every time. Then you get him in the fourth quarter and he's dumping the ball off to the fullback. Those guys are hard to scout.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: Getting your opponent into a third-and-seven is like doubling down on an eleven when the dealer is showing a six. You know you should win the hand, and even when you don't, you have confidence you made the right play.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: People want to tell you that defense wins championships, or some such formula that satisfies announcers who have ten seconds to deliver digestible analysis. But defense and offense are pretty much equally important. Putting a team together isn't easy.
author: Joey Berglund, Coach, Tennessee Raftsmen

text: We are what we pretend to be, so we must be very careful what we pretend to be.
author: Kurt Vonnegut

text: If you don't absolutely love every aspect of a certain player and you're sitting in the first couple of rounds with a pick on your hands, either trade down or get the best defensive lineman on your board. You can always use another good defensive lineman in the mix.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: To run the ball successfully, you need a quick-footed center. Teams that run well have one.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: Ambition is easy. What makes players great is succeeding in the face of others' ambitions.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: The league is full of too many offensive linemen who can't adjust to a simple line call. We have more rules protecting the quarterback these days because if we allowed hits like we did 20 years ago, not a single one of them would make it through all 16 games.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: Good decisions are significantly more difficult than what you'll find in a sportswriters notebook. There are no shortcuts in this league, and someone making $15,000 a year isn't going to find an answer that we haven't considered and analyzed.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: Having a good quarterback is easily worth 15-20% of your salary cap. At least half of that is guaranteed money. It's not a stretch to say that a GM who makes a bad quarterback decision will soon be unemployed.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Never confuse a single defeat with a final defeat.
author: F. Scott Fitzgerald

text: I would love to put the free safety back in a man-1 and dare them to throw the ball, but quarterbacks are so much more accurate these days, and every receiver can hurt you. Our corners would get flagged on every play.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: Linebacker is a great position for a normal-sized guy who simply loves the game and is willing to sacrifice everything to get out there on the field. But there are a lot of them out there, so the minute that fire dies in them, you can find another one.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: GMs make mistakes when they sign underachievers. You're probably not getting the guy you want if you find an underachiever and he wants a lot of money up front.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: Every once in a while you're tempted to remind a player what it means to put on that jersey. But this is pro ball, and he gets paid to wear it. If he doesn't hear 60,000 fans and he doesn't see their home town in block capitals on his shirt, the time for reminding him has long passed.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: The first thing you do when you're picking near the top of the draft is you make sure you have a quarterback. If you don't know, chances are you don't have one.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: It's tempting to make a huge splash on the first day of free agency. You can always renegotiate your way into affording that one big offer. But you'll miss out on adding depth to your team after those few huge contracts are signed. And everybody has injuries.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: Maneuver yourself into a position of strength and you'll quickly find that everyone wants to bring you down.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: The man in black flew along the sidelines, and the gunslinger rained fire.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: I don't know if there's such a thing as a shutdown corner in this league anymore. With the way the passing game has grown, I think those days are in the past.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: You need to throw deep every once in a while just to keep them honest. But if you don't have the arm or the receiver, then when? Either you risk an interception, or you put your offense in a hole.
author: Nick Carraway, Coach, Buffalo Snow Riders

text: You run hard at the combine and you play in those all-star games because everything you make your first three years in the league depends 100 percent on where you're drafted.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Failure is part of success, an integral part. Everybody gets knocked down. Knowing it will happen and what you must do when it does is the first step back.
author: Bill Walsh

text: When you have a 16-game season, everyone's banged up. Durability isn't about never missing a play, it's knowing how to play when you're hurting.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: Both winning and losing quarterbacks completed just over 21 passes per game last season. But winning quarterbacks did it with 10.4 incompletions and 0.5 interceptions, while losing quarterbacks did it with 13.1 incompletions and 1.2 interceptions. Mistakes lose games.
author: Mac McLeod, League Statistician

text: Winning on the road is not about what the crowd does during the game. It's about what you do to prepare your team for not being able to hear while you have the ball.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: People sometimes ask me what I'm looking for after the draft, when we sign a bunch of guys who weren't drafted. I'm still looking for players. If drafting were perfect, we wouldn't need any more. Immediately after the draft is when we admit we often have no idea what we're doing.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Power running is a lost art. Once the buffalo roamed the plains, and there was safety in the herd. Then we started spending more on defensive linemen and we thinned out the herd.
author: Anthony Patch, Coach, Washington Landslides

text: More teams have moved to the 3-4, but they just don't have the bodies to pull it off. It's hard to find 325-to-330 pounds who can move.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: Sometimes the easiest interceptions are the hardest ones. You just can't help taking that peek to see where you're going to run. And it's off your hands and everyone in the stadium gasps at once. Like you let the air out of every tire in the entire parking lot.
author: Louis Holland, Philadelphia Condors

text: We're not playing fantasy football here.
author: Bill Polian

text: The only character issues that truly matter are whether you think he's capable of taking coaches and teammates seriously and whether he's capable of learning and executing plays. That adds up to a lot, both on and off the field.
author: Cass Timberlane, GM, Cincinnati Tigers

text: The only reason I ever get mad is if we've worked on a particular read in practice. I throw the ball knowing he's going to turn around when he sees he's covered deep. And he goes anyway and it's an easy pick.
author: Adam Trask, Pittsburgh Ironmen

text: The beauty of overpaying for the top of the first round is that no one tanks games in our league. Scouting isn't perfect, so there's no incentive to break the budget for a player who probably isn't the answer fans expect to hear after you just went 2-14.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: Being a kicker is a lot like being a golfer. Every time you hit the ball, it has to fly the right way. That takes a lot of practice. I think there would be more golfers taking up football if only we didn't get run over by a 250-pound linebacker every time we kicked it short.
author: Amory Blaine, Miami Bay Sharks

text: Coaches who can outline plays on a blackboard are a dime a dozen. The ones who win get inside their player and motivate.
author: Vince Lombardi

text: What is a red flag? What makes us unwilling to draft or sign a player, even if we feel he's the best available at his position? That's a good question and I think it varies from team to team and from year to year.
author: George F. Babbitt, GM, Arizona Pioneers

text: Great receivers have no humility. Great quarterbacks possess both humility and resolve.
author: Donald Kenyon Hill, Poet Laureate of Football

text: We tend to ignore special teams when we're building a roster. Maybe that's a mistake. Coaches stress that coverage is all about staying in your lane, and that's true, but there's value to the young players who will do it at full speed every time.
author: Homer Wells, GM, Minnesota Norsemen

text: Some people look at touchdowns to interceptions, but that's more a function of the game plan. I look at yards per attempt, mostly. And I want to look at every interception in the red zone. That's where you'll see the serious flaws.
author: Dick Diver, Coach, Oakland Bandits

text: I can't tell you how many players look like they are all-world until the pads come on.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: A great play-action quarterback is much more limited when he's behind.
author: Hurston Wood, Coach, Seattle Cyberhawks

text: Some of the greatest minds in the sport have absolutely no idea what to look for in a college wide receiver. I'm telling you, quarterback is hard to scout, but with receivers you might as well just throw darts at a dart board.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars

text: The better pass defenders these days are bigger and stronger. You can't hit receivers early, and you can't hold them up too much, so you need to make it count right at the line and right when they're going up for the ball.
author: Clyde Griffiths, Coach, New York Gladiators

text: When it comes to evaluating intelligence, the Solecismic test is a very small piece of the evaluation puzzle. Professional teams value football intelligence much more highly than book smarts.
author: Benj Steinberg, Coach, Baltimore Nightwings

text: We used to go into every game with 15-20 scripted plays on offense. No changes. Then defenses caught on and started blitzing early, knowing we weren't scripting long passes. So now we have a hot read built into just about every pass play.
author: Eugene Witla, Coach, Jacksonville Cougars

text: You have to spend a lot of money or do a truly fine job in free agency if you want to overcome a bad draft.
author: Milt Dagget, GM, New Orleans Delta Rays

text: Ain't nothing wrong with reading a playbook by the light of a jukebox.
author: Ken Stabler

text: Say what you will about the sweet miracle of good pass defense. If you give a decent quarterback enough time, he will find an open receiver. Good coverage these days is very time-sensitive.
author: Joey Berglund, Coach, Tennessee Raftsmen

text: You might have 15 players rated with the same grade. If it is close, you might go with the need, but it has to be very close.
author: Ernie Accorsi

text: You know it when an offensive coordinator is limited by his quarterback. He's calling plays he thinks will work for his quarterback and not the best plays to attack his opponent.
author: Frank Cowperwood, Coach, Detroit MoCats

text: There are many starters at this level who received an opportunity by default, not because they earned it. The best GMs know the difference, and don't make the mistake of giving the wrong players starter money in free agency.
author: George Milton, GM, St. Louis Stags

text: The teams that are consistently good at what they do are those that come away from the draft with talent.
author: Doremus Jessup, GM, New England Colonials

text: In baseball, you know what you're getting when you see that a player hit 40 home runs last season. There's no mystery. In football, you're dealing with a lot more than an occasional curveball. I think I would actually get eight hours of sleep if I were in baseball.
author: Will Kennicott, Coach, Dallas Lone Stars
